points.text: Set client timeout slightly lower than the upstream service SLA to fail fast and avoid long hangs.
Implement exponential backoff with full jitter for retries to spread load and prevent retry storms.
Cap the number of retries and use a circuit breaker to stop retries after repeated failures.
Make all retryable calls idempotent or include unique request IDs for safe deduplication.
Track latency histograms; adjust timeout values based on the 99th‑percentile observed latency.
On the network, investigate packet loss and congestion causing TCP retransmissions (ping, traceroute, sFlow).
Check NIC and OS TCP settings (RTO min/max, retransmission limits) match your latency expectations.
Deploy health‑checking load balancers to bypass unhealthy instances, reducing cascading timeouts.
Set client timeout = expected service latency + network margin; keep it shorter than upstream timeout to avoid cascading delays.
Apply exponential backoff with jitter for retries to spread load and prevent thundering herd.
Introduce a circuit breaker that opens after N failures, halting retries until service recovers.
Cap the maximum number of concurrent retries per endpoint to limit overload on the downstream service.
Ensure all retried calls are idempotent or use request IDs to avoid duplicate processing.
Monitor TCP retransmission rates; high values indicate packet loss or congestion needing network investigation.
Check for latency spikes, packet loss, and MTU mismatches on the path between services.
Tune TCP parameters (RTO, max retransmissions) and enable keep‑alive to improve connection stability.
Implement load shedding or fallback responses when downstream latency exceeds acceptable thresholds.
Set client timeout shorter than upstream timeout to avoid cascading retries.
Use exponential backoff with jitter for retries to reduce thundering‑herd effect.
Introduce a circuit‑breaker that opens after N consecutive failures and backs off.
Configure load balancer idle‑timeout and max‑connections to prevent queue buildup.
Enable TCP keepalive and tune net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time to detect dead peers early.
Monitor kernel retransmission counters (netstat -s, /proc/net/snmp) for spikes.
Check SYN backlog (net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog) and increase if drops occur under load.
Verify interface error counters and packet loss using ethtool or ifconfig.
Ensure MTU consistency and enable Path MTU Discovery to avoid fragmentation.
Review load balancer health‑check intervals; make them aggressive enough to drop slow backends quickly.
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for retries to avoid thundering herd on a slow dependency.
Set a circuit breaker that opens after N consecutive timeouts, halting further retries until recovery.
Cap the maximum number of retries per request to prevent unbounded load during outages.
Use connection pooling with TCP keepalive to reuse sockets and reduce handshake latency.
Monitor TCP retransmission rates, RTT, and RTT variance to detect congestion or packet loss spikes.
Check for bufferbloat on intermediate routers/switches that can increase latency and cause retransmissions.
Verify that TCP window scaling and socket buffer sizes are tuned for the expected bandwidth‑delay product.
Enable ECN on hosts and network devices to get early congestion signals before packet loss occurs.
Inspect path MTU and fragmentation issues that may cause silent packet loss and retransmissions.
Correlate application timeout settings with observed network RTT to avoid premature timeouts.
Allocate timeout budget: total SLO latency minus upstream latency estimates and a safety margin.
Use exponential backoff with jitter for retries; limit max attempts to prevent thundering herd.
Cap concurrent retries per client or per endpoint to protect downstream services.
Monitor TCP retransmission rates and RTT; spikes indicate network congestion or packet loss.
Check for asymmetric routing, MTU mismatches, and proper TCP window scaling on both sides.
Deploy a circuit‑breaker that halts retries when error rate exceeds a defined threshold.
